heading: LXVI SAVERIO CAMPANINI
card_summary: This chunk is entirely Campanini's introduction to the De Harmonia Mundi, covering two main topics. First, he concludes his analysis of Zorzi's threefold argumentative structure, showing how Proclus's Elementatio theologica governs the logic of emanation (from the One to ten, read through the Sefirot as mensurae/middot, citing the Sefer Yetzirah tradition and Abraham ben Alexander of Cologne's Keter Shem Tov), and how the third cycle of argument closes with the Proclian doctrine of return, culminating in the mors osculi — the Talmudic "death by kiss" that Zorzi, following Pico della Mirandola, defines as "estasi o rapimento." Second, Campanini catalogues Zorzi's encyclopedic sources, listing dozens of authors from Homer, Hesiod, and the Platonists (Plotinus, Porphyry, Iamblichus, Proclus) through the Church Fathers (Origen, Augustine, Pseudo-Dionysius, Bonaventure, Aquinas) to Arab philosophers (Al-Farabi, Averroes, Avicenna) and astrological writers, noting that Zorzi deliberately avoids citing contemporaries, with the rare exceptions of Nicholas of Cusa and Dante.
essay:
## Overview

Pages 66–70 belong entirely to Saverio Campanini's extended critical introduction rather than to Zorzi's text itself. They fall into two well-defined movements: (1) the final stage of Campanini's exposition of Zorzi's concentric argumentative circles, culminating in the Kabbalistic concept of the *sefirot* and the mystical *mors osculi*; and (2) a panoramic survey of the sources deployed throughout the *De Harmonia Mundi*, framed by a quotation from John Donne.

---

## The Third Argumentative Circle: Sefirot, Emanation, and Return

Campanini traces the innermost circle of Zorzi's argument to the Kabbalistic doctrine of the *sefirot*, introduced in the context of Neoplatonic emanation. The first emanation produces the first number; following Proclus, the One then generates ten — and here, Campanini notes, the sole authority Zorzi invokes is the *Theologia Hebraeorum*, i.e., authentic Kabbalah:

> la prima emanazione genera il primo numero, e qui Proclo continua a fare da guida al ragionamento zorziano, dall'uno poi viene emanato il dieci, e per questo non sorregge altro che la 'Theologia Hebraeorum', ovvero la qabbalah autentica

<cite>p. 66</cite>

Critically, Campanini observes that in the *De Harmonia Mundi* — unlike the later *Problemata* and the commentary on the *Elegante Poema* — Kabbalah appears not as specific textual citation but as a *communis opinio cabbalistica*, an anonymous deposit of archaic tradition coeval with primordial revelation. This explains the difficulty in identifying precise sources for many of Zorzi's original Kabbalistic ideas.

The *sefirot* are interpreted in this passage as *mensurae* (Latin) or *middot* (Hebrew), rendering them as "dimensions" or "measures" — a reading rooted in the oldest commentaries on the *Sefer Yetzirah* and transmitted through the *Keter Shem Tov* of Abraham ben Alexander of Cologne. Campanini notes that this identification of *sefirot* with *mensurae* is the common property of the Kabbalistic tradition:

> le sefirot possono essere descritte ricorrendo al termine middot traducibile appunto con mensurae ovvero dimensioni

<cite>p. 66–67</cite>

Elsewhere in the *De Harmonia Mundi* Zorzi interprets the same *sefirot* as "sfere" (spheres), a term that would have fit equally well in the present Hermetic context. Campanini stresses that no single source can be assigned for this — it is shared ground.

---

## The Helical Structure of Zorzi's Rhetoric

Campanini introduces a striking metaphor for the formal architecture of the *De Harmonia Mundi*: its argumentative circles are not merely concentric but *helical* — spiraling inward and upward simultaneously, so that each cycle revisits the same territory at a deeper level of meaning:

> Il movimento elicoidale che i cerchi concentrici della prosa zorziana sviluppano è l'unico che permette al suo autore di argomentare in modo ricorrente e, allo stesso tempo, ascendente o, se si preferisce, penetrando strati sempre più intimi di significato.

<cite>p. 68</cite>

The first circle draws on pagan antiquity (the image of Pan); the second, pivoting on the Ineffable Name and the arithmetical contemplation of its letters, moves through Plato–Boethius–Augustine to Christian foundations — constituting first a *Praeparatio* and then a *Demonstratio*. The third, innermost circle reaches the *sefirot* and closes with the Proclian doctrine of return (*epistrophê*), which governs not only Zorzi's cosmology but the very form of his prose.

---

## Mors Osculi: The Mystical Climax

The culminating concept of this innermost circle is the *mors osculi* — the "death of the kiss," drawn from Talmudic tradition (b. Berakhot, Mo'ed Katan), the prerogative of the purest souls. Following Pico della Mirandola, Zorzi defines this state as *estasi o rapimento*, made possible by the:

> "separazione dell'anima dal corpo, senza che il corpo sia divelto dall'anima"

<cite>p. 68</cite>

Campanini identifies this as an irreducible paradox that captures the persistent fascination and perpetual fragility of Zorzi's mystical and encyclopedic journey — beyond imagination, this side of the senses.

---

## The Sources of the De Harmonia Mundi: An Encyclopedic Catalogue

The second major movement of this chunk opens with a quotation from John Donne's prose, which cites "Francis George" (Zorzi) on the proportional correspondence between Noah's Ark and the human body — a detail Zorzi discovered, to his annoyance, had already been identified by Saint Augustine. This anecdote emblematizes Zorzi's encyclopedic ambition and his anxiety of precedence.

Campanini then presents what amounts to a *tabula praesentiarum* for the *De Harmonia Mundi*, arguing — with apparent paradox — that it would be easier to list absences than presences, since the sources tend toward the totality of available learning. He connects this bibliographic impulse to Alfredo Serrai's suggestion that Christian Kabbalah is linked to the birth of modern bibliography.

The catalogue Campanini enumerates is remarkable in its range:

- **Greek and Latin literary authors**: Homer, Hesiod, the tragedians, Plutarch, Philo, Josephus, Philostratus, Plautus, Horace, Ovid, Martial, Juvenal, Pliny, Cicero, Virgil, Lucan, Silius Italicus, Vitruvius, Varro, Apuleius, Gellius, Censorinus, Macrobius
- **Ancient and late-antique philosophers**: Pythagoras, Plato, Aristotle, Theophrastus, Sextus, Dio Chrysostom, Plotinus, Porphyry, Iamblichus, Amelius, Synesius, Julian the Apostate, Themistius, Damascius, Simplicius, Alcinous, Speusippus, Proclus, Syrianus
- **Church Fathers and medieval scholastics**: Basil, Origen, Eusebius, Lactantius, Chrysostom, Cyril of Alexandria, Pseudo-Dionysius, Boethius, Jerome, Ambrose, Augustine, John of Damascus, Gregory the Great, Bonaventure, Roger Bacon, Ramon Llull, Duns Scotus, Anselm of Aosta, Thomas Aquinas (cited sparingly), Peter Lombard, Bernard of Clairvaux, Richard and Hugh of St. Victor, Albert the Great (cited frequently), Giles of Rome, Robert Grosseteste, Francis of Meyronnes
- **Arab philosophers**: Al-Farabi, Al-Ghazzali, Averroes
- **Medical authorities**: Hippocrates, Galen, Isaac Israeli, Avicenna, Giovanni Serapion, Constantine the African, Pietro d'Abano
- **Astrological writers**: Ptolemy, Hyginus, Manilius, Firmicus Maternus, Vettius Valens, Al-Kindi, Ali ibn Ridwan, Masha Allah

> una galleria o, per meglio dire, un'enciclopedia delle grandi voci del passato

<cite>p. 69</cite>

---

## The Conspicuous Absence of Contemporaries

Campanini draws attention to a deliberate authorial strategy: citations of near-contemporaries are sparse, often disguised under the formula *novitiores* ("more recent authors"). The rare explicit exceptions are Nicholas of Cusa (cited once) and Dante Alighieri. The most recent historical figure named in his own person is Giovanni da Capistrano, placed at the dramatic Battle of Belgrade in 1456. Campanini reads this as reflecting the superior prestige of ancient authority and Zorzi's wish to operate on the rarefied heights of metaphysical speculation rather than in the lowlands of academic disputation.

The section closes with Campanini's methodological note: the commentary to the Italian translation has sought, within limits deemed useful, to trace also those sources Zorzi does not explicitly name — whether in the body of the text or in its margins.

---

## Technical Terms Introduced

- **Sefirot** (Hebrew: ספירות) — the ten divine emanations; interpreted here as *mensurae* / *middot* (Hebrew: מידות, "measures/dimensions")
- **Mors osculi** (Latin) — "death of the kiss," Talmudic concept of the death of the righteous; *estasi o rapimento* in Zorzi's Pichian rendering
- **Epistrophê** (Greek: ἐπιστροφή) — Proclian doctrine of return, governing both cosmological and rhetorical structure
- **Communis opinio cabbalistica** — Campanini's term for Zorzi's use of Kabbalah as shared anonymous tradition rather than specific textual citation
- **Tabula absentiarum** — Campanini's ironic formula for the impossibility of cataloguing what Zorzi *does not* cite
